questions.prompt: A tornado chart helps you identify:
choices: The probability that NPV is positive
Which inputs the project NPV is most sensitive to
questions.explanation: Tornado charts vary one input at a time and rank by NPV range. They tell you where to focus due-diligence effort; correlations and probabilities require scenarios or Monte Carlo.

questions.prompt: Worst/base/best scenarios produce NPVs of -$25M, +$40M, +$150M with probabilities 0.25/0.50/0.25. What is the expected NPV? Answer in $M.
Which limitation is unique to scenario analysis (vs tornado charts)?
choices: It collapses a continuous distribution of outcomes into a few discrete points
questions.explanation: Scenarios respect correlations (their advantage over tornado) but compress the outcome distribution to 2-3 points. Monte Carlo addresses both limitations.

questions.prompt: Which patterns should make you reconsider a project even though base-case NPV is positive?
choices: Knife-edge NPV that swings from +$20M to −$20M with ±5% input changes
Break-even revenue growth that's far above the historical sector growth rate
IRR comfortably above cost of capital
Discount rate just barely above the IRR
questions.explanation: Fragility, unrealistic break-even, and razor-thin risk-adjusted returns are all red flags. A solid IRR above cost of capital is good news, not a warning sign.
